agenda_item_basics.title_of_item.answer: Authorization to Purchase Replacement Single-Axle Plow Truck for Streets Division

submission_details.submitted_by.answer: Michael M. Larson, Public Works Director

submission_details.presenter_name_title.answer: Michael M. Larson, Public Works Director

issue_description.summary_of_issue.answer:
The Public Works Department is requesting City Council approval to purchase one (1) replacement single-axle plow truck for the Streets Division to maintain core winter maintenance and street operations.

Background
The Streets Division currently operates a fleet of eight (8) primary plow trucks that are used for snow and ice control, spring street sweeping, and summer hauling of aggregate, asphalt millings, and other materials. Unit #27, a 2007 International 7400 single-axle plow truck with approximately 167,000 miles and 10,800 engine hours, has reached the end of its useful life. Over the past three winter seasons, Unit #27 has experienced increasingly frequent mechanical failures, including hydraulic system leaks, transmission slipping under load, and intermittent electrical issues affecting the lighting and salting controls.

In the 2025–2029 Capital Improvement Plan previously reviewed by the City Council, replacement of this unit was programmed for 2026, contingent upon budget availability and final Council authorization. The 2026 adopted budget includes funding for this replacement in the Public Works Capital Equipment line item.

Procurement Process
City staff requested and received pricing through the Minnesota State Cooperative Purchasing Venture (CPV) program, which provides pre-bid, competitively awarded state contracts for vehicles and equipment. Staff obtained a detailed quote from Boyer Trucks of Duluth for an International HV507 SFA chassis with a Henderson dump body, underbody scraper, front plow, wing plow, and hydraulic controls, configured to match the City’s existing equipment standards. Staff also requested a comparison quote from Nuss Truck & Equipment for a Mack equivalent, but the Mack configuration came in approximately $19,000 higher with longer lead times.

The recommended package from Boyer Trucks, including chassis, body, plow package, stainless steel sander, and all necessary lighting and safety equipment, totals $293,475.62 before tax. After applicable sales tax and estimated delivery fees, the not-to-exceed project cost is $313,000.00. The quote is attached to the agenda packet.

Operational Need
During the 2024–2025 winter season, Unit #27 was out of service on five separate occasions during active snow events, resulting in delayed plowing in the northeast quadrant of the city, particularly in the Oak Street and Mississippi Parkway neighborhoods. In two events, staff had to reassign a truck from the south route, creating longer plow times and multiple resident complaints regarding timeliness and ridged windrows at driveways.

Maintenance staff have advised that continuing to operate Unit #27 will likely result in increased downtime and escalating repair costs, especially related to the transmission and hydraulic system. The truck’s frame and box also show significant corrosion from years of winter salt exposure, and the truck no longer meets staff’s preferred safety standard for primary deployment on steep or heavily trafficked routes when fully loaded.

The new truck will improve reliability, reduce time to complete snow routes, and allow the department to maintain its current service level objectives: primary streets cleared within 8–10 hours of a typical storm, and residential streets within 12–16 hours. The stainless steel box and updated hydraulic system should also reduce long-term maintenance costs compared to the existing unit.

Fleet Management and Disposition
If approved, the new unit would be ordered in March 2026 with an estimated delivery timeline of 10–12 months based on vendor lead-time estimates. During that period, staff will continue to operate Unit #27 as a backup, focusing it on lighter-duty assignments when possible.

Once the new truck is placed into service, Unit #27 will be removed from the primary fleet. Staff intends to declare Unit #27 surplus property and dispose of it via public auction through the City’s standard surplus process. Proceeds from the sale will be returned to the Equipment Replacement Fund.

In summary, the requested purchase is consistent with the adopted Capital Improvement Plan, is funded in the 2026 budget, and is necessary to maintain safe and efficient snow and ice control operations throughout the City.

issue_description.alternatives_options_e.answer:
Alternatives Considered
1. Defer Replacement by 1–2 Years
Staff considered deferring replacement of Unit #27 for an additional one to two years. While this would temporarily reduce capital spending, it carries several risks:
- Increased Repair Costs: Over the last 24 months, the City has spent approximately $18,400 on major repairs to Unit #27, including hydraulic pump replacement, brake system repairs, and electrical troubleshooting. The Fleet Supervisor estimates at least $8,000–$12,000 in near-term repairs if we continue to operate the truck through two more winters, not including unanticipated failures.
- Reliability and Service Impacts: When Unit #27 is down, we either lengthen routes for neighboring trucks or bring in a smaller backup unit that is not optimized for heavy snow. This has already led to extended plow times and complaints from residents about delayed service and narrower cleared lanes.
- Safety Concerns: The truck’s older braking system and corroded frame rails increase staff concern when operating on steep grades, particularly on the west side hills and near the industrial park. While the truck is still passable for limited-duty use, it is not ideal as a primary plow unit.

2. Purchase a Used Plow Truck
Staff also looked at the used market through regional dealers and state surplus listings. Two challenges emerged:
- Limited Availability: There are relatively few late-model used municipal plow trucks available in Minnesota, and those that are available are often heavily used with high miles and hours, similar to our current unit.
- Unknown Maintenance History: Used trucks typically do not come with detailed maintenance records or manufacturer warranties. Acquiring a used unit could create a situation where the City is inheriting someone else’s deferred maintenance problems, leading to higher lifetime costs and more downtime.
- Upfitting and Compatibility: A used chassis may require significant upfitting to match our existing plow controls, lighting, and hydraulic systems, which can reduce any initial savings.

3. Outsource Additional Plowing to Private Contractors
Another option is to contract out one additional snow route to a private contractor instead of replacing the truck immediately.
- Cost Uncertainty: Contractor rates fluctuate by season and storm frequency. Staff estimates that contracting a full route could cost $40,000–$70,000 per winter, depending on snowfall. Over several years, this is likely to exceed the cost of owning and operating a City unit.
- Control and Accountability: While we do use contractors for cul-de-sacs and certain alleys, relying on a contractor for a full primary route can reduce our control over response times and quality. Residents typically call the City, not the contractor, when there are issues, and it is more difficult to adjust routes and priorities in real time.
- Equipment and Training: Our operators are familiar with City equipment and routes, and we have standardized training and safety protocols. Relying more heavily on contractors introduces variability in equipment condition and operator experience.

Effects on Others
- Residents and Businesses: A new, reliable plow truck helps ensure that residential neighborhoods, school zones, and commercial corridors are cleared in a timely and consistent manner. This reduces the likelihood of school bus delays, employee commute issues, and access problems for local businesses during and after snow events.
- Public Safety: Police, fire, and ambulance services depend on passable streets to respond quickly to emergencies. During the February 2025 snow event, for example, a medical call on Mill Avenue was delayed by approximately four minutes when responders encountered partially plowed streets and had to slow significantly. While multiple factors contributed, having a full complement of functional plow trucks helps minimize these situations.
- Public Works Staff: Frequent breakdowns require operators to switch trucks mid-shift and mechanics to prioritize emergency repairs over scheduled maintenance. This can lead to overtime, schedule disruption, and increased stress during already demanding snow operations.

Comments
From a long-term asset management perspective, the recommended purchase aligns with the City’s strategy of replacing heavy equipment on a planned cycle before it becomes a chronic maintenance liability. The stainless steel box and newer emissions and hydraulic systems on the proposed unit should extend its useful life and reduce corrosion issues compared to the 2007 truck.

Staff also notes that ordering the truck now, rather than delaying, helps us secure a build slot and avoid potential price increases associated with steel costs, chassis availability, and supply chain disruptions. The vendor has indicated that prices on similar units increased between 5–8% over the last two years.

In conclusion, while alternatives exist, staff believes they either shift costs into more unpredictable operating expenses, reduce service reliability, or introduce additional risk and uncertainty. Replacing Unit #27 at this time is the most responsible and cost-effective approach for maintaining the City’s snow and ice control operations.

issue_description.recommended_action_mot.answer: Motion to adopt a resolution authorizing the purchase of one (1) International HV507 SFA single-axle plow truck with Henderson dump body, stainless steel sander, underbody scraper, and plow package from Boyer Trucks of Duluth through the Minnesota State Cooperative Purchasing Venture in an amount not to exceed $313,000.00, including sales tax and delivery, to be funded from the 2026 Public Works Capital Equipment budget (Equipment Replacement Fund), and to authorize staff to declare Unit #27 (2007 International 7400 plow truck) as surplus equipment and dispose of it via public auction upon successful delivery and commissioning of the new unit.

financial_impact.please_explain.answer:
The total not-to-exceed cost of $313,000.00 for this purchase is included in the adopted 2026 budget under the Public Works Capital Equipment (Equipment Replacement) line item. The City’s 2025–2029 Capital Improvement Plan anticipated replacement of this unit in 2026, and the corresponding appropriation was approved by the Council as part of the 2026 budget adoption.

Funding will come from the Equipment Replacement Fund, which is supported by annual internal service charges to the operating departments. The current fund balance is sufficient to cover this purchase without additional levy impact or interfund borrowing. No grants are being used for this purchase, and no debt issuance is required.

Upon disposal of Unit #27 through public auction, any proceeds received (estimated between $8,000 and $15,000 based on comparable units sold in the region) will be returned to the Equipment Replacement Fund to partially offset the capital outlay. Actual proceeds will depend on market conditions and unit condition at the time of sale.

Annual operating costs (fuel, routine maintenance, and insurance) are expected to be similar to or slightly lower than the current unit due to improved fuel efficiency and reduced unscheduled repairs. These operating costs are already accounted for within the Streets Division operating budget. Therefore, this request does not create a new ongoing operating expense beyond what is already planned; it replaces an aging asset with a newer, more reliable unit within the existing fleet size.
